home.title: Ricky Martinez - Home
home.tagline: Don't Repeat Yourself. Engineer with Precision.
home.name: RICKY MARTINEZ
home.description: Developer and problem-solver who thrives on efficiency, creativity, and automation. From test frameworks to full-stack systems, I love building solutions that make technology work smarter, not harder.
home.aboutText1: I'm a NYC-based software engineer with a passion for building efficient, automated solutions. When I'm not coding, you'll find me exploring the city or tinkering with something new.
home.aboutText2: I have a chronic interest in developing software and optimizing workflows. Whether it's network automation, QA automation, or personal life automation, I enjoy finding patterns and creating solutions that make things easier.
home.aboutText3: Born in Queens and raised in the Bronx, I've always been curious about how things work. This curiosity led me to learn music, engineering, and eventually, software development. Now I focus on building automation tools and efficient systems—because why not be efficient?
home.aboutText4: This site itself uses automation I've developed for both backend and frontend. Check out the

descriptions.blue-manager: Property maintenance app with OCR and Supabase backend.
descriptions.personal-website: Personal site to host notes, tutorials, and projects — originally built as a Python teaching platform.
descriptions.xpress-transit: Ride management and scheduling app built with Django and React for handling vehicle and passenger data.
descriptions.auth: Simple Python-based authorization script that enables or restricts features based on username permissions.
descriptions.connectivity-monitor: Monitors network connectivity via ping, TCP, or UDP and alerts when endpoints become unreachable.
descriptions.cr-ip-lookup: Utility for performing IP and hostname lookups to quickly identify and cross-reference connected resources.
descriptions.error-checker: Automated script validator that scans all scripts for runtime errors and emails daily success or failure reports.
descriptions.f5-sdk: Wrapper script that uses the F5 SDK to extract and store network device configurations and metadata.
descriptions.f5-diffs: Compares F5 configuration snapshots to identify and log differences between environments or revisions.
descriptions.f5requests: Lightweight Python library for easily interacting with F5 devices over REST APIs.
descriptions.f5-vip-search: Search utility for locating and analyzing Virtual IP (VIP) configurations across multiple F5 load balancers.
descriptions.gen-graph: Python module for generating charts and graphs from structured data to embed in dashboards or web pages.
descriptions.g-photography-php: A PHP-based photo gallery and management system with enhancements over the original Python version.
descriptions.interface-err-report: Tool for parsing and reporting interface errors across network devices to assist with troubleshooting.
descriptions.intranet-site: Internal company intranet built to centralize scripts, dashboards, and documentation for quick access.
descriptions.ip-requests: Toolkit for logging and analyzing IP-based HTTP requests with metadata and reporting utilities.
descriptions.kappasigmahc: Static website and code repository for the Kappa Sigma High Council chapter, showcasing events and history.
descriptions.muscribe: Early prototype of an AI-powered tool that converts instrument audio into sheet music or note notation.
descriptions.gops: C++ prototype of a navigation system designed to process coordinates and simulate GPS-like pathing logic.

gitTopics.introText: Git is an incredibly useful tool that is used for version control. It is a standard and is commonly used by web developers to keep track of changes made in a project. The repository has two areas: staging, working:
gitTopics.workingArea: working area - this is your local directory where files are being changed and testing
gitTopics.stagingArea: staging area - where changes are kept, ready to be pushed into the repository

post1.title: How Living With MS Changed the Way I Engineer Software
post1.featuredDescription: Exploring the nuances of building under constraints—from determinism and recovery to automation and accessibility.
post1.p1: I'm a senior automation engineer living with multiple sclerosis.
post1.p2: MS introduces variability in energy, cognition, and physical strength and consistency. Some days are sharp, where I feel like I can solve any hard technical problem. Some require pacing because my brain decided 'nope'.
post1.p3: That variability changed how I think about engineering systems. I no longer design for ideal conditions. I design for constraints and the "unexpected".
post1.p4: And that has fundamentally improved the way I build software.
post1.p5: Recently, I ran into a frustrating issue with my CI pipeline on one of my repos.
post1.p6: Two GitHub Actions jobs were running Playwright tests simultaneously. Locally, everything passed. In CI, tests started failing intermittently.
post1.p7: At first glance, it looked like selector flakiness.
post1.p9: The real issue was resource contention — concurrent jobs binding to the same ports, spinning up browsers simultaneously, creating race conditions that only appeared under shared runner constraints.
post1.p10: The problem wasn't "fix the selector," it was:
post1.blockquote1: Design the system such that it behaves predictably under constrained resources.
post1.p11: Living with MS makes you sensitive to variability. You stop assuming stability and start engineering for degradation.
post1.p12: That mindset made the difference in debugging the pipeline.
post1.p13: When cognitive energy fluctuates, unpredictability becomes expensive.
post1.p14: Non-deterministic pipelines, unclear error messages, hidden state transitions — they all increase cognitive tax.
post1.p15: So I have a strong bias toward determinism.
post1.p16: In my Playwright tests, I ensured:
post1.li1: Explicit waits instead of implicit timing assumptions.
post1.li4: Reduced parallelism when resource contention matters.
post1.blockquote2: Deterministic systems reduce mental overhead. They preserve energy — for both humans and machines.
post1.p18: Automation is often framed as productive, fast responses towards repetitive tasks.
post1.p20: When I built my personal site, I didn't want manual content maintenance. So I automated it.
post1.p22: Instead of manually updating my projects page:
post1.li8: A GitHub Action queries the GitHub API.
post1.p24: Automation protects against inconsistency — especially when energy fluctuates or brain fog hits.
post1.p25: At one point, I was journaling directly into my site.
post1.p26: But writing raw HTML was time consuming and cumbersome.
post1.p27: So I created a lightweight markup system tailored to how I write notes.
post1.li18: A predictable syntax aligned with my thinking patterns.
post1.p29: Then I built a script to:
post1.p30: Why? Because reducing friction, steps, and simplifying processes matters.
post1.p31: When cognitive load increases, even small formatting decisions become noise. Designing a system aligned with my thought process removed unnecessary mental tax.
post1.p32: That's not just accessibility — it's being proactive.
post1.p34: But accessibility is a system property.
post1.blockquote3: That's accessibility. A system that handles degraded network conditions is accessible. A workflow that survives interruption is accessible. An API that tolerates retries is accessible.
post1.p36: Living with neurological variability makes this obvious.
post1.p40: You ask: "What happens when it partially works?"
post1.p42: You ask: "Is this recoverable? What happens if it fails mid-flight?"
post1.p43: In my automation scripts and pipelines, I build for recovery:
post1.p44: Because failure isn't rare. It's normal.
post1.p46: Living with MS hasn't reduced my engineering capability.
post1.p52: Engineering from constraint produces stronger systems.
post1.p54: MS introduced variability into my life.
post1.p55: So I build software that assumes variability too.
post1.blockquote4: And systems that assume variance are more resilient — for everyone.
post1.p56: I'm currently exploring engineering roles focused on automation, testing, and accessibility. If this perspective resonates, I'd welcome a conversation.

post2.title: Accessibility Is Not Just a Feature
post2.featuredDescription: Why treating accessibility as infrastructure, not an afterthought, leads to more resilient systems—and better engineering for everyone.
post2.p1: When most teams talk about accessibility, it's usually an afterthought in the process.
post2.li4: Run a WCAG audit before release
post2.p3: These are all important because they frame accessibility as a surface-level feature — something that sits on top of the product.
post2.p4: Living with multiple sclerosis and working as an automation engineer, I've come to believe something different:
post2.blockquote1: Accessibility isn't just a feature. It's something to be decided as infrastructure.
post2.p5: And when it is seen that way, the entire way system design is approached, changes.
post2.h2Problem: The Problem With Treating Accessibility Like a Feature
post2.p6: During planning, Feature thinking assumes a "normal" user.
post2.p7: That's when accessibility becomes an exception to that normal case.
post2.p8: You build the system for the majority, and then you patch the edges. But real systems don't behave that way.
post2.li10: in environments that were never imagined
post2.p10: When systems fail under those conditions, the issue isn't accessibility — the issue is fragility.
post2.p12: When engineers talk about infrastructure, we talk about resilience:
post2.li13: systems that continue working under constraint or pressure
post2.p13: Accessibility is the same principle applied to users. A resilient system assumes:
post2.p14: If a product only works under perfect conditions, it isn't robust. It's just optimized for the ideal scenario.
post2.p15: Living with MS means dealing with cognitive fatigue. Some days your brain processes information slower than usual.
post2.p16: Complex interfaces that might feel manageable to others suddenly become overwhelming.
post2.p17: This changes how you evaluate software.
post2.li18: deeply nested menus or convoluted and confusing UIs
post2.li20: error messages that provide no clear action and leave you wondering "what?"
post2.li21: systems that force you to remember steps instead of guiding you
post2.p19: These aren't just UX annoyances, they are an increase in cognitive load and infrastructure.
post2.p20: When software requires excessive mental processes, it excludes users long before a screen reader ever even enters the picture.
post2.h2Engineering: Engineering Systems With Accessibility in Mind
post2.p21: Thinking of accessibility as infrastructure changes the engineering priorities. You begin designing for constraint first, not just an afterthought.
post2.p23: Systems should have predictable and clear transitions.
post2.p25: Ambiguous system states create confusion, distraction and increase cognitive load.
post2.p26: Error messages should answer four questions immediately:
post2.li28: What can I do about it?
post2.p27: Non-deterministic messages like "Something went wrong" force users to guess what went wrong.
post2.p28: A reliable system doesn't assume perfect connectivity or ideal scenarios. Basements, elevators and older buildings often have poor signal.
post2.p29: If a task completely fails because the network dropped, that system isn't accessible in the real world and a fallback is necessary. Graceful degradation, indeed, matters.
post2.p30: Automation reduces the number of decisions users need to make by automating tasks to increase efficiency.
post2.p32: Consistency isn't just a design preference — it's also infrastructure.
post2.p33: The interesting thing about infrastructure improvements towards accessibility is that they help everyone.
post2.p34: The same design choices that help someone with a disability also help:
post2.li29: a tired developer using the tool at 2 AM
post2.li30: someone working on a slow laptop
post2.li31: a user with a bad internet connection
post2.li32: a person learning the system for the first time
post2.p37: When accessibility is treated as a feature, teams tend to address it at the end of development or if there is time left in the sprint.
post2.p38: When accessibility is treated as infrastructure, it becomes a must-have from the beginning, not a nice-to-have.
post2.li33: How does this behave when conditions aren't ideal?
post2.li34: What happens when the user is tired or distracted?
post2.li35: Does the system guide the user, or does it make them think harder?
post2.li36: What if the user doesn't use the system the way it's designed to?
post2.p40: The goal isn't just compliance. The goal is robustness.
post2.p41: Robust systems work for more people, in more environments, under more conditions. That's good engineering.

post3.title: Operating Under Constraint: Job Search With MS
post3.featuredDescription: Job search with multiple sclerosis: cognitive load, interview prep, using AI as a devil's advocate, and framing automation as feedback systems—not memorization.
post3.p1: There was a company restructure. The initial momentum of a job search — the urgency, the structure and discipline it forces on you — carried me for a bit. Then it slowed. Follow-ups not following up. A market that was tougher than I originally expected, with more competition and fewer responses than the volume of applications.
post3.p2: That alone is demoralizing in the specific, cumbersome way that a long search gets demoralizing. But there was something else.
post3.p3: MS doesn't pause for circumstances. Cognitive fatigue doesn't check whether you have a recruiter screen before deciding to show up. Some days the working memory just isn't there — reading the same job description or documentation three times and retaining less each time, losing the thread mid-sentence on something you've said dozens of times before, sitting down to prep and finding that mental sharpness just isn't there. Not broken. Not incapacitated. But not sharp either, and sharpness is what interviews reward.
post3.p4: The search wasn't happening in a vacuum. It was happening inside a body with its own schedule, running alongside a market with its own indifference. That combination required a different approach — not because I was trying to compensate, but because the standard approach wasn't viable. Burning through limited cognitive reserves on memorization isn't a strategy. It's just expensive and fragile.
post3.p5: Early in the search I was preparing the way most candidates do: rehearsing answers, listing technologies, anticipating questions. It's a reasonable starting point but a poor finishing point.
post3.p6: When I picked up that a team ships roughly three to four major releases per year, I didn't just log or store that, I planned around it. Low release frequency means high-stakes releases. High-stakes releases mean testing confidence is critical. That means QA isn't just a speed function — it's a risk management function. The team isn't optimizing for shipping fast; they're optimizing for not breaking things when they do ship.
post3.p7: That single inference changed how I framed every technical answer. I stopped talking about test coverage as output and started talking about it as signal quality. The question isn't whether tests exist — it's whether they tell you something trustworthy at the moment you need to make a decisive call.
post3.h2AiPrep: How I Used AI in Prep
post3.p8: I want to be specific here because "I used AI to prep" can mean anything from generating a script to actually sharpening your reasoning.
post3.p9: I used it as a devil's advocate, not a ghostwriter. I'd describe my positioning and ask where it was weak. I'd draft a technical answer and ask what a skeptical interviewer would push back on. I'd lay out an assumption I was making about the role and ask whether there was evidence to actually support it.
post3.p10: The goal was to be challenged before the interview, not validated. Validation is useless in this context. What I needed was to find the gaps in my reasoning while I still had time to close them.
post3.p11: This mattered more than it might for someone without cognitive variability in the picture. On a good day I can hold a complex technical narrative in my head, stress-test it internally, and identify where it's thin. On a harder day that process is slower and less reliable — things that feel coherent in the moment turn out to have gaps I didn't catch. Using an external tool to test my thinking meant I wasn't depending entirely on my own working memory to find the flaws.
post3.p12: The structure it helped most with was separating facts from interpretations. I had real data points — the release cadence, some signals from the job description, things that came up in the hiring manager conversation. AI helped me be rigorous about which conclusions those data points actually supported versus which ones I was filling in from assumption. That discipline — treating delays as normal rather than rejection, not over-interpreting recruiter behavior — was something I had to work on explicitly. MS fatigue and a long search are the perfect combination for spiraling on ambiguous signals. Having a structured way to check my reasoning helped interrupt that.
post3.p13: When I talked about my technical work, I wasn't positioning myself as someone who writes tests. I was positioning myself as someone who improves feedback systems.
post3.p14: The distinction is concrete. I reduced CI runtime from 13–15 minutes down to 2–5 minutes through test sharding and smarter parallelization. That's not just a testing improvement. It changes how quickly engineers get feedback after a change, which changes how they work, which changes how many small errors make it further downstream than they should.
post3.p15: I talked about API-first testing strategy as a way to get durable, fast signal without the fragility of full end-to-end tests for every case. I talked about CI as a feedback loop with a latency problem, not a quality gate that you bolt on at the end.
post3.p16: The framing I kept coming back to: automation is an engineering discipline, not a support function. The infrastructure around software development is as worth designing carefully as the software itself.
post3.h2Background: What Was Happening in the Background
post3.p17: While I was doing this preparation, I was also managing logistics that most candidates don't have to think about.
post3.p18: Accessibility considerations for an in-office interview. Optimizing interview timing — late morning is usually my best cognitive window, so that's what I asked for. Thinking through how much to disclose, and when, without either oversharing early or creating surprises later.
post3.p19: These aren't complaints. They're constraints. And like any engineering constraint, they required design decisions rather than just effort. You don't push through a resource limitation by trying harder — you navigate around it intelligently and efficiently.
post3.p20: The energy management piece in particular affected how I structured prep sessions. I couldn't do four-hour marathon preparation blocks. What I could do was focused, well-scoped sessions with clear objectives and real stopping points. That limitation forced a kind of preparation discipline that I think actually produced better results than brute-force rehearsal would have.
post3.p21: I'm not treating anything as the finish line. It's another data point in a process, and the outcome is partly outside my control. What's inside my control is the quality of my thinking, the clarity of my communication, and the accuracy of how I represent what I've actually built and learned.
post3.p22: The search isn't over. But something has shifted in how I approach interviews — and honestly, how I think about positioning technical work more generally. Reading systems rather than memorizing answers is a transferable skill. It's one I'll keep using regardless of how this particular process ends.
post3.p23: From the "look at it and figure out what's wrong," from my parent when I was younger and would take electronic appliances apart to figuring MS out – what works and what doesn't work.
post3.p24: Progress doesn't always announce itself. Sometimes it's just: you're thinking differently than you were four weeks ago, and that counts.

pythonTutorial.title: Python Tutorial
pythonTutorial.subtitle: Learn the basics of programming in Python. Start from the beginning and work your way through each lesson.

lesson0.title: Introduction

lesson0.subtitle: Welcome to the Python Tutorial series

lesson1.title: Setting Up
lesson1.description: Install Python and PyCharm on Windows or Mac
lesson1.subtitle: This one is going to be annoying.

lesson2.title: Hello World!
lesson2.subtitle: Extremely short but remember, this is an intro still
lesson2.createProjectText: Start up PyCharm and create a new Project and name it PythonTutorial

lesson3.title: Conditionals
lesson3.description: Learn if/else statements and control flow
lesson3.subtitle: If this is true, then this happens/is true.
lesson3.smallIntroText: Conditionals are a control structure. Control structures are used to change the flow of the code to execute instructions.
lesson3.ifExplanation: The if statement is one of the simplest control structures. Assuming condition is a valid condition, if the condition evaluates to true, then instruction executes. If condition evaluates to false, instruction is skipped over and doesn't execute (if else condition isn't specified/ present)
lesson3.elseExplanation: The else statement is used with the if statement. Else is used to tell the code what to do if condition is False.

lesson4.title: Loops
lesson4.subtitle: While condition is true, keep doing this. (This one may be long)
lesson4.smallIntroText: Instructions in programming are normally executed sequentially (in the order they're written). There are, however, times where a certain block of code needs to be executed multiple times. In this instance, a loop is required.

codingChallenges.title: Understanding "My" Way
codingChallenges.quote: if you can't explain it in your own words, you don't really know it.
codingChallenges.description: The following are in categories instead of "lesson" number. There will also be a little explanation/ description to explain the code/concept.

trie.title: Trie
trie.intro: Tries are very useful and are useful when implementing an autocorrect feature.
trie.example1: For exmaple, on the left we start with no letter. But then we branch of into the letters t, A, i. Let's focus on the t for now.
trie.example2: t branches off into to and te. to is its own word (for all that we know) so that's it for that specific word.
trie.explanation1: Lets understand that a bit, just in case:
trie.explanation2: te banches off into those words (child nodes) because, for all we know, those are the words that can be formulated using that te (parent node).

aiTutorial.docTitle: AI for Engineering Automation — Ricky Martinez
aiTutorial.title: AI for Engineering Automation
aiTutorial.subtitle: Where AI helps in engineering workflows, how to pick a model, and patterns I use for automation.
aiTutorial.introP1: This guide is opinionated and practical: it reflects how I use AI as an automation engineer—not a catalog of every tool on the market.
aiTutorial.introP2: Treat every model as a probabilistic component: it can speed you up or quietly ship subtle mistakes. The sections below are the decision points I run through before leaning on AI for a task.
aiTutorial.hWhere: Where to implement AI (and where not to)
aiTutorial.whereP1: Good candidates share a few signals: repetition, clear inputs, tolerance for a first draft, and a human or automated check before anything reaches production.
aiTutorial.whereLi1: Boilerplate and scaffolding: test data, config stubs, migration sketches, repetitive CRUD—then you review and harden.
aiTutorial.whereLi2: Explaining and decomposing: turning logs, stack traces, or legacy scripts into structured notes or a test plan.
aiTutorial.whereLi3: Refactoring with guardrails: “same behavior, smaller functions” works when tests or typecheckers catch regressions.
aiTutorial.whereP2: Poor fits: anything that needs a guaranteed correct answer with no verification (auth, billing, security policies), or work where the cost of a wrong word is higher than the cost of doing it slowly yourself.
aiTutorial.captionWhere: High level: use AI when you can verify outputs cheaply.
aiTutorial.whereP3: If you cannot name what would falsify a bad answer (a test, a diff, a policy check), pause before outsourcing the thinking.
aiTutorial.toolsP1: Capabilities overlap, but I reach for different products for different cognitive loads. Names and UIs change; the split below is about how I use them today.
aiTutorial.toolsP2: None of these replace domain judgment—they change how fast you draft, search, and stress-test ideas.
aiTutorial.tableCaption: AI tools compared for engineering use
aiTutorial.chatgptUse: General explanations, rewriting, quick scripts, brainstorming product copy, turning rough notes into checklists.
aiTutorial.chatgptWatch: Overconfidence on APIs you did not paste; vague “best practices” without your stack context.
aiTutorial.claudeUse: Longer code review–style reads, comparing diffs, algorithmic reasoning, structured refactors across many files when I paste enough context.
aiTutorial.claudeWatch: Still hallucinates imports and edge cases—verify with run/test.
aiTutorial.copilotUse: In-IDE completion, small localized edits, boilerplate from surrounding symbols—fastest when the editor already has the repo open.
aiTutorial.copilotWatch: Tunnel vision: it optimizes the current file, not the system.
aiTutorial.perplexityUse: Grounded-ish lookup when I want citations to docs or release notes, not when I need executable code without checking.
aiTutorial.perplexityWatch: Stale or wrong sources; always open the primary link.
aiTutorial.toolsP3: When a task spans “explain the approach” and “edit the repo,” I often start in Claude or ChatGPT for structure, then finish in the IDE with Copilot/Cursor for local syntax and repetition.
aiTutorial.patternsP1: These are the patterns that have held up across my projects: they assume CI, linters, or another verification gate exists somewhere in the loop.
aiTutorial.patternsLi1: Spec → generate → verify: paste constraints and interfaces; generate a script or test; run it in a sandbox before merging.
aiTutorial.patternsLi2: Devil’s advocate: ask explicitly for failure modes, race conditions, and “what would break this in CI?” on a PR you already wrote.
aiTutorial.patternsLi3: Documentation from truth: generate README or runbook sections from the actual commands in your pipeline file—never the other way around.
aiTutorial.patternsLi4: Incremental prompts: one coherent step per message (repro command, expected vs actual, hypothesis) beats a wall of context.
aiTutorial.patternsP2: The goal is not maximum tokens; it is minimum surprise when you run that automation again six months later.
aiTutorial.diagramWhere:
flowchart TD
  subgraph in_signals [Inputs]
    T[Task description]
    A[Artifacts: code, logs, specs]
  end
  in_signals --> Q{Repeatable or exploratory?}
  Q -->|Repeatable draft| D[AI-assisted draft]
  Q -->|Exploratory design| E[AI brainstorm then narrow]
  D --> V{Automated checks possible?}
  E --> V
  V -->|Yes| OK[Iterate with tests / linters / CI]
  V -->|No| H[Prefer manual or pair review]
aiTutorial.diagramSequence:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Engineer
    participant AI as Model
    participant CI as CI / tests
    Dev->>AI: Context + constraints + desired artifact
    AI-->>Dev: Draft (code, test, or doc)
    Dev->>Dev: Review + local run
    Dev->>CI: Push with checks
    CI-->>Dev: Pass / fail signal
    Note over Dev,CI: AI output is untrusted until checks green
aiTutorial.captionSequence: Human and automated gates around generated work.
aiTutorial.promptsIntro: Use plain structure; models mirror the bullets you give them.
aiTutorial.promptBlock1:
Goal: (one sentence)
Environment: (OS, runtime, versions)
Inputs / outputs: (files, APIs, env vars)
Constraints: (no network, idempotent, must run in CI)
Deliverable: (language) script + how to run + exit codes
aiTutorial.promptBlock2:
Change summary: (what you did)
Risk focus: (data integrity, concurrency, security)
Question: List the top 5 ways this could fail in production and how to test for each.
aiTutorial.footerNote: I will extend this page over time—splitting topics into separate lessons if the workflows grow.
